_**User**_

I want to create an educational project that try to replicate my municipality in Belgium. We will gradually build features in this project. 

I've a specific stack that I want to use. 

In the document @IDEA.md I've asked another AI to think as the head of the population departement of a Belgian muncipality to give me a big idea of how it works and how we should implement things.

I want you, now, as an expert architect, being a bit biaised because I've the stack which is the follwoing: 

Technology stack:

- .NET 10 (latest)
- C#
- Blazor Web App
- MudBlazor
- Minimal API
- Vertical Slice Architecture
- Domain-Driven Design (lightweight)
- FluentValidation
- EF Core
- SQLite for development
- PostgreSQL later
- MediatR only if you think it adds value (otherwise avoid unnecessary abstraction)

to create some documentations first about this project; to sketch something out of that documents and the stack I told you. 

As I said, it's an educational projet but I want it to grow a lot, so really think clean, with tests also, with a correct architecture separate, etc

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Only IDEA.md exists in the project. I am beginning to draft comprehensive architecture documentation based on the Belgian municipality replication requirements and the specified vertical slice stack.

I am mapping out the full documentation structure. The plan includes eight files covering README, architecture, domain models, tech stack, roadmap, testing strategy, and development guidelines.

A glossary of Belgian municipality terminology will be added to the documentation set. The architecture will center on a Blazor Web App with MudBlazor and a Minimal API. Vertical Slice Architecture will organize features by folder rather than by layers. Lightweight DDD will use aggregates, value objects, and domain events only where they add real value. MediatR will be used sparingly, since Minimal API can call handlers directly without unnecessary abstraction.

MediatR will be omitted initially. Each feature slice will follow a direct Endpoint → Handler → Persistence flow. Validation and logging will be handled through FluentValidation and direct handler invocation instead of pipeline behaviors.

The concrete directory layout is now defined. The solution will contain four projects under `src/`: a Web application hosting both the Blazor frontend and Minimal API, a Domain project for core aggregates and entities, an Application project for cross-cutting concerns, and an Infrastructure project for EF Core and external services. Tests will live in a dedicated `tests/` folder split across Domain, Integration, and Web test projects. MediatR will be introduced later only if repetitive pipeline behaviors such as logging, validation, or authorization become necessary.

The Web project will contain a Features folder. Each slice lives in its own subfolder under Features. The slice contains the endpoint handler validator and request. The domain and infrastructure projects remain separate.

The bounded contexts from IDEA.md are Reception Identity Immigration Residence Address Household and Registration.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

I've turned your `IDEA.md` domain narrative and your stack into a documentation set that sketches how this project should grow.

## What was created

| Document | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| [README.md](README.md) | Project overview, principles, planned solution layout |
| [docs/ARCHITECTURE.md](docs/ARCHITECTURE.md) | VSA + lightweight DDD, projects, slices, state machine, MediatR stance |
| [docs/DOMAIN.md](docs/DOMAIN.md) | Bounded contexts, aggregates, use-case mapping from IDEA phases |
| [docs/TECH-STACK.md](docs/TECH-STACK.md) | Stack rationale, packages, EF/SQLite→PostgreSQL, what to avoid |
| [docs/ROADMAP.md](docs/ROADMAP.md) | 10 phases from scaffold to exception scenarios |
| [docs/TESTING.md](docs/TESTING.md) | Pyramid, per-layer strategy, naming, CI |
| [docs/GLOSSARY.md](docs/GLOSSARY.md) | Belgian admin terminology → code enums |

## Architectural choices (biased toward your stack)

**Three projects, not five** — `Domain`, `Infrastructure`, `Web`. Feature slices live in `Web/Features/{Context}/{UseCase}/` so you don't get a ceremonial `Application` layer.

**`RegistrationCase` is the spine** — The four core questions from `IDEA.md` map to a explicit state machine (`Draft` → … → `Registered` / `Rejected` / `Suspended`). Invalid transitions throw domain exceptions.

**MediatR: skip for now** — Direct `Endpoint → Handler → Repository` is easier to learn and debug. Add MediatR only when cross-cutting pipelines repeat across many slices.

**Blazor Server + direct handler injection** — Same process as Minimal API; no HTTP round-trip during desk workflows. API routes stay for tests and future clients.

**Tests from day one** — Domain tests for invariants, integration tests per slice, API tests for HTTP contracts. Builders in `Domain.Tests/Builders/`.

## Suggested build order

```
Phase 0  → Solution scaffold, MudBlazor shell, SQLite, health check
Phase 1  → Open case + record identity (first vertical slice end-to-end)
Phase 5–6 → Police loop + approval (complete first-registration story)
Phase 8  → IDEA exceptions (EU vs non-EU, duplicates, etc.)
```

## Solution shape (preview)

```
src/
├── SchaerbeekMunicipality.Domain/           # Aggregates, VOs, events — zero infra deps
├── SchaerbeekMunicipality.Infrastructure/   # EF Core, repositories, stubs
└── SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/            # Blazor + Minimal API + Features/
tests/
├── SchaerbeekMunicipality.Domain.Tests/
├── SchaerbeekMunicipality.Integration.Tests/
└── SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web.Tests/
```

---

The repo is documentation-only right now. If you want to continue, I can scaffold **Phase 0** next: solution, projects, MudBlazor layout, EF Core + SQLite, and the first `RegistrationCase` aggregate stub with a smoke test.

_**Agent (default)**_

Documentation now includes **.NET Aspire** as the orchestration layer. Summary of what changed:

## Key decision

| Concern | Approach |
|---------|----------|
| **Local dev** | Run `AppHost` → Web + PostgreSQL container + Aspire dashboard |
| **Tests / CI** | Host `Web` directly with SQLite — no AppHost, no Docker |
| **Deploy (Phase 9+)** | `aspire publish` instead of hand-written Docker Compose |

This keeps `dotnet test` fast in CI while daily development uses a real PostgreSQL instance.

## New projects in the solution

- **`SchaerbeekMunicipality.AppHost`** — orchestrator (default start project)
- **`SchaerbeekMunicipality.ServiceDefaults`** — OpenTelemetry, health checks, HTTP resilience

## Files updated

- **README.md** — stack summary, solution layout, start command
- **docs/TECH-STACK.md** — full Aspire section (AppHost shape, packages, config, deployment)
- **docs/ARCHITECTURE.md** — diagram, dependency rules, future stub services as Aspire resources
- **docs/ROADMAP.md** — Phase 0 and Phase 9 aligned with Aspire
- **docs/TESTING.md** — tests explicitly bypass AppHost

## Start command (once scaffolded)

```bash
dotnet run --project src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.AppHost
```

Phase 0 will scaffold the Aspire solution (`dotnet new aspire-starter` or equivalent, adapted to your VSA layout). Want me to proceed with that scaffold next?

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user asks whether GitHub Actions CI is suitable for their public project.

GitHub Actions is free for public repositories with generous limits. Their test setup uses SQLite and avoids Docker, making it ideal for CI without AppHost containers.

Docker support is available on GitHub Actions runners if they add PostgreSQL later. .NET 10 may be released by the project timeline. The response will cover the workflow structure and public repository security best practices.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Yes — **GitHub Actions is a very good fit** for this project, especially as a **public** repo.

## Why it works well for your setup

Your docs already steer CI in the right direction:

- **Tests use SQLite + `WebApplicationFactory`** — no AppHost, no PostgreSQL container for the default pipeline
- That means a simple workflow on `ubuntu-latest` is enough: `dotnet restore` → `dotnet build` → `dotnet test`
- **No Docker required** for Phase 0–8 CI, which keeps runs fast and reliable

Aspire stays a **local dev** concern; CI validates the app logic without orchestrating containers.

## Public repo = free CI

For public repositories on GitHub:

- **Actions minutes are free** (with fair-use limits that are generous for a typical educational project)
- No cost concern for build + test on every push/PR
- You get branch protection, required checks, and PR status badges for free

That’s one of the main reasons public open-source .NET projects use GitHub Actions.

## What a minimal workflow looks like

```yaml
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          dotnet-version: '10.0.x'
      - run: dotnet restore
      - run: dotnet build --no-restore
      - run: dotnet test --no-build --verbosity normal
```

Phase 0 in your roadmap already targets exactly this.

## Things to watch (public repo hygiene)

| Topic | Recommendation |
|-------|------------------|
| **Secrets** | Never commit connection strings; CI uses SQLite via test factory overrides |
| **Aspire / PostgreSQL** | Only add Docker/Testcontainers in CI when you explicitly want PG validation (Phase 9+) — optional nightly job, not every PR |
| **Fork PRs** | GitHub doesn’t expose repo secrets to forks by default — fine, since you don’t need secrets for build/test |
| **Badges** | Add a CI badge to README once the workflow exists |

## Optional later: heavier CI

When you reach Phase 9, you *can* add a second job with Testcontainers PostgreSQL on `ubuntu-latest` (Docker is available on GitHub-hosted runners). Keep it **optional or nightly** so PR feedback stays fast.

---

**Bottom line:** GitHub Actions + public repo + SQLite-based tests is a clean, standard, zero-cost setup for this educational project. Aspire doesn’t block CI at all.

If you want, I can add a `docs/CI.md` and a `.github/workflows/ci.yml` skeleton when we scaffold Phase 0.

## Created

**`.github/workflows/ci.yml`**
- Runs on push/PR to `main`
- Sets up .NET 10, installs Aspire workload (needed to build AppHost)
- `dotnet restore` → `dotnet build` (Release) → `dotnet test` (Release)
- Cancels stale runs when new commits land on the same branch

**`docs/CI.md`**
- What runs in CI and what intentionally does not (no AppHost, no PostgreSQL)
- Local parity commands
- Status badge template for after you publish on GitHub
- Branch protection tips
- Public repo notes (free minutes, no secrets needed)
- Phase 9 optional PostgreSQL/Testcontainers job (future)

- **README.md** — CI doc link + commented badge placeholder
- **ROADMAP.md** — CI item marked done (workflow exists; will pass after Phase 0 scaffold)
- **TESTING.md** / **TECH-STACK.md** — link to `CI.md`

## Note

CI will fail until the solution exists (Phase 0). Once you scaffold the projects, the first green run should appear automatically on push.

After publishing to GitHub, uncomment the badge in `README.md` and replace `YOUR_GITHUB_USER`.

_**Agent (default)**_

**No — a separate Minimal API `/health` route is not needed** with Aspire. That was redundant in the roadmap; the docs are updated to reflect that.

## What Aspire already gives you

**ServiceDefaults** (via `AddServiceDefaults()` + `MapDefaultEndpoints()`) registers and exposes:

| Endpoint | Role |
|----------|------|
| `/health` | Readiness — registered checks must pass |
| `/alive` | Liveness — process is running |

The **Aspire dashboard** adds orchestration-level monitoring (Web project, PostgreSQL container, logs, traces). That’s separate from the app’s own `/health` endpoint.

## What you still use Minimal API for

Business use cases only, under something like `/api/registration/cases` — not infrastructure health.

## What you might add later (Phase 9)

A **database health check**, not a new route:

```csharp
builder.Services.AddHealthChecks()
    .AddDbContextCheck<AppDbContext>();
```

That plugs into the existing `/health` pipeline from ServiceDefaults.

## Smoke test

The Phase 0 integration test can still call `GET /health` — it hits ServiceDefaults, not a hand-written endpoint. `WebApplicationFactory` runs in Development by default, which is when `MapDefaultEndpoints()` exposes those routes.

---

**Roadmap Phase 0** now says: wire ServiceDefaults for health, scaffold Minimal API for **business routes only**. See the new “Health checks vs Minimal API” section in [ARCHITECTURE.md](docs/ARCHITECTURE.md).

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'm thinking through a critical review of the architecture, identifying both what works well—like the consistent terminology and pragmatic testing approach—and where there are genuine concerns, starting with the SQLite testing strategy.

The biggest technical risk is using SQLite for integration tests while developing against PostgreSQL. Migrations written for Npgsql often won't run on SQLite due to naming conventions, unsupported column types like `citext`, and operations like `ALTER COLUMN` that SQLite doesn't handle. The docs claim "same EF model and migrations; provider selected by configuration," but that's rarely true in practice. I'd need to either maintain separate migration sets, use `EnsureCreated` for SQLite tests, or just run Testcontainers with PostgreSQL from the start instead of trying to support both.

The state machine design is also problematic—the status flow is presented as strictly linear (Draft → IdentityPending → ResidenceCategoryPending → ...), but real intake processes collect data in any order. The naming is semantically backwards too: `IdentityPending` should mean "waiting for identity data," but the test shows it as the state *after* recording identity, which is confusing. A checklist model tracking data completeness flags would be clearer than this rigid pipeline.

There's also a syntax issue in the C# examples where `case` is used as a variable name, which won't compile since it's a reserved keyword.

The Aspire workload installation step is outdated—as of Aspire 9 in December 2024, the workload is no longer required and the SDK comes from NuGet instead. The CI and tech stack docs still reference the old workload install command.

Health endpoints are only exposed in Development mode by default, but Phase 9 deployments would need them in non-dev environments. Also worth noting that FluentAssertions v8 and MediatR both moved to commercial licensing models in 2024-2025, though FluentAssertions remains free for open source projects. The docs treat these as free add-later options without mentioning the licensing changes.

There's some design ambiguity around the repository pattern mixing in unit-of-work concerns—multiple repositories in a single handler could lead to multiple SaveChanges calls. The Web.Tests and Integration.Tests projects have overlapping scope since both use WebApplicationFactory, so they could potentially be consolidated. The project also lacks a CONTRIBUTING guide and architecture decision records, which would be helpful for an educational codebase meant to grow. There's no actual LICENSE file yet despite the docs mentioning educational use—a real license like MIT should be added for a public repository. The GLOSSARY entry for "A Card" needs verification on the exact Belgian residence permit classification.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

I've read all eight documents end to end. Overall verdict: **the documentation is coherent, well-scoped, and unusually disciplined for a side project** — but there are a few real technical problems buried in it, one of which will bite you in Phase 0 or 1 if not resolved. Here's my honest review.

## What's genuinely good

- **The docs agree with each other.** Terminology, folder layout, state names, and phase numbering are consistent across all seven files, and cross-links are correct. That's rare.
- **Scope discipline.** The "what we avoid" sections (no MediatR, no generic repositories, no CQRS, no event sourcing) with explicit *conditions for revisiting* are exactly right for an educational project that wants to grow. Most projects either over-engineer day one or never write down when to add things.
- **The roadmap is demoable.** Every phase ends with something you can show and test, and the slice checklist template is a nice touch.
- **The four core decisions from IDEA.md** are threaded through everything — README, DOMAIN.md's gate table, the Phase 6 review dashboard. The domain narrative wasn't just pasted in; it was actually digested.

## Problems worth fixing

**1. The SQLite-for-tests / PostgreSQL-for-dev split is riskier than the docs admit.** TESTING.md claims "same EF model and migrations; provider selected by configuration." In practice this often breaks: migrations generated for Npgsql contain PostgreSQL-specific SQL (snake_case naming applied at migration time, PG column types) that SQLite cannot execute, and SQLite doesn't support several migration operations at all (e.g. most `ALTER COLUMN`). You have three honest options — use `EnsureCreated()` for SQLite tests (skipping migration validation), maintain awareness that tests validate the *model* but not the *migrations*, or just use Testcontainers PostgreSQL for integration tests from the start. The docs should pick one explicitly instead of implying it will just work. My recommendation: SQLite + `EnsureCreated()` for the fast suite, and pull the Testcontainers PG job forward from Phase 9 to whenever you write your first real migration.

**2. The state machine semantics are backwards in places.** TESTING.md has this example: `RecordIdentity_WhenDraft_MovesToIdentityPending`. Recording identity should *clear* a pending state, not enter it. More broadly, the strictly linear pipeline (`IdentityPending → ResidenceCategoryPending → AddressDeclared → DocumentsPending → …`) doesn't match how intake actually works — an officer collects identity, address, and documents in whatever order the conversation goes. Consider fewer statuses (e.g. `Intake → AwaitingPoliceVerification → UnderReview → Approved/Rejected/Suspended → Registered`) plus a computed **completeness checklist** for the four core decisions. DOMAIN.md already hints at this ("decision screen aggregates checklist flags computed from domain state") — the checklist model and the linear status model are in mild tension; I'd resolve it in favor of the checklist.

**3. Code samples use `case` as a variable name.** `RegistrationCase case` appears in ARCHITECTURE.md and TESTING.md. It's a C# reserved keyword and won't compile. Trivial, but these are the samples you'll copy from later — rename to `registrationCase`.

**4. The Aspire workload instructions are outdated.** Since Aspire 9, there is no workload — Aspire ships as NuGet packages and an SDK reference; `dotnet workload install aspire` is deprecated. Both TECH-STACK.md and CI.md (plus the actual `ci.yml` step) reference installing the workload. On .NET 10 that CI step will either no-op or fail. The workflow should drop it.

**5. Licensing changes in your chosen libraries.** Two of the named packages went commercial: **FluentAssertions v8+** (free only for non-commercial/OSS — fine for this project, but worth pinning intent or noting v7 stays Apache) and **MediatR** (paid licensing since 2025). The MediatR section reads as if it's a free add-later option; if you ever do want the pipeline pattern, the built-in alternative is writing a small `IRequestHandler` abstraction yourself, which is arguably more educational anyway. Also, the docs say "NSubstitute or Moq" — just say NSubstitute; picking one avoids inconsistency and sidesteps Moq's SponsorLink history.

## Smaller observations

- **Glossary accuracy:** some card descriptions look wrong — e.g. the **A Card** is a limited-duration residence permit for non-EU nationals, not "proof of registration as EU citizen" (EU citizens get the E/EU card, which is missing from the table). For an educational project this matters; I'd add a disclaimer that descriptions are simplified and verify the card table against official sources before encoding it as enums.
- **`Web.Tests` vs `Integration.Tests` boundary is fuzzy.** Both use `WebApplicationFactory`; TESTING.md describes them as separate layers, but "handler test" and "API test" will overlap heavily. Consider merging into one integration test project with folders — three test projects is ceremony the architecture doc otherwise argues against.
- **Repository owning `SaveChangesAsync`** works fine with one aggregate per handler, but the docs don't say what happens when a handler touches two aggregates (e.g. `RecordPoliceResult` updates both the request and the case). Worth one sentence: either share the `DbContext` unit-of-work or accept a single `SaveChanges` at the end.
- **No actual license.** README says "educational use" but a public GitHub repo without a LICENSE file is legally "all rights reserved." Pick MIT (or similar) before publishing.
- **Missing docs that would fit this project's ambitions:** a `CONTRIBUTING.md` (even just for future-you), and lightweight **ADRs** (architecture decision records). You've already made real decisions with rationale — no MediatR, SQLite-for-tests, health via ServiceDefaults — capturing each as a one-page ADR would be more durable than prose scattered across files, and it's a good habit to learn.
- **Health endpoints in Development only:** ARCHITECTURE.md correctly notes ServiceDefaults maps `/health` only in Development. Phase 9's deployment will need them exposed (with caching/restrictions) in production too — the Phase 9 checklist could mention that.

## Bottom line

The documentation is in better shape than most real projects I see — the main structural bet (VSA + lightweight DDD + Aspire, monolith with contexts as folders) is sound and well-argued. Before you scaffold Phase 0, I'd fix items 1–4: decide the test-database strategy honestly, simplify the state machine, fix the `case` samples, and remove the Aspire workload step from CI. The rest can be cleaned up as you go.

Want me to apply those fixes to the docs?

_**Agent (default)**_

All review fixes are applied. The final grep confirms no stale references remain — the only mentions of the old state names and the Aspire workload are intentional (the ADR documenting why the design changed, and notes explaining the workload is no longer needed).

## What changed

**Test-database strategy (the big one)** — The docs now state honestly that migrations are PostgreSQL-specific and cannot run on SQLite. The fast suite uses SQLite + `EnsureCreated()` (validates model and behavior); a separate Testcontainers PostgreSQL CI job (`Category=PostgreSQL`) validates migrations, pulled forward from Phase 9 to Phase 1 when the first real migration is created. Updated in `TESTING.md`, `TECH-STACK.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `ROADMAP.md`, and `CI.md`.

**State machine redesign** — Replaced the linear pipeline with a small status machine (`Intake → AwaitingPoliceVerification → UnderReview → Approved → Registered`, plus `Rejected`/`Suspended`) and a **computed completeness checklist** (`IdentityEstablished`, `LegalResidenceEstablished`, `AddressConfirmed`, `RegisterDeterminable`). Data collection updates flags; only decisions change status. This fixed the backwards `RecordIdentity → IdentityPending` semantics too.

**Aspire workload removed** — `ci.yml` no longer runs `dotnet workload install aspire` (deprecated since Aspire 9; packages ship via NuGet). `CI.md` and `TECH-STACK.md` explain why.

**Compile-safety and licensing** — `case` renamed to `registrationCase` in all samples; FluentAssertions v8 license note added (with the v7 Apache-2.0 fallback); MediatR section now flags its 2025 commercial licensing and recommends hand-rolling a pipeline if ever needed; Moq dropped in favor of NSubstitute alone.

**Glossary corrections** — A/B cards fixed (limited/unlimited non-EU stay), F card corrected to family member, H card to EU Blue Card, added EU/EU+ and K/L cards with the 2021 renaming noted, plus an accuracy disclaimer.

**Structural cleanups** — `Web.Tests` merged into `Integration.Tests` (two test projects total, reflected in README, TESTING, ROADMAP); a note in `ARCHITECTURE.md` clarifies multi-aggregate handlers commit atomically via the shared `DbContext`; Phase 9 now includes exposing health endpoints outside Development.

## New files

- `LICENSE` — MIT. **Replace "SchaerbeekMunicipality project contributors" with your actual name** before publishing.
- `docs/adr/` — four Architecture Decision Records (no MediatR, test-database strategy, health via ServiceDefaults, checklist over linear state machine) plus an index with a template for future decisions. Linked from the README docs table.

The documentation set is now internally consistent with today's decisions. Next natural step remains scaffolding Phase 0 whenever you're ready.
